sections.title: What is the Nominative Case?
sections.description: In German, the Nominative Case is the "naming" case. Think of it as the default setting for a word before any action (like giving, seeing, or owning) starts to change its role. It is primarily used for the subject of the sentence—the person or thing performing the action.

sections.title: 1. Articles, Adjectives, and Nouns
sections.description: The ending of an adjective changes depending on whether it follows a definite article (the), an indefinite article (a), or no article at all. This is called adjective declension.

translations.label: Neuter
translations.translation: Nice child, come here! (Used when addressing directly without an article).

content.key_rule: If the word "the" (der/die/das) is already there to show the gender, the adjective takes a simple -e ending (except in plural). If there is no "the" to show the gender (like with ein), the adjective must "carry" the gender marker (e.g., ein netter Vater because der ends in -r).

sections.title: 2. Special Determiners
sections.description: Certain words act exactly like the definite article (der/die/das). If you use these, the adjective will always end in -e (singular) or -en (plural).

example.note: Jeder acts like der, so gut just gets an -e.

sections.title: 3. Pronouns
sections.description: Pronouns replace nouns. In the nominative, they represent the subject.

personal_pronouns.title: Personal Pronouns (The "Who")
personal_pronouns.description: These are the basic subjects of any sentence:

dependent.label: Dependent
dependent.explanation: Used before a noun (e.g., My dog).

independent.label: Independent
independent.explanation: Used alone (e.g., The dog is mine).

sections.title: Summary of Usage
sections.description: You use the nominative system when:

content.note: In German, "to be" acts like an equals sign (A = B), so both sides are Nominative.
